edges.description: Impaired pathway flux causes accumulation of pathway intermediates upstream of the defective enzymatic step.
In acute hepatic porphyrias, inherited heme-enzyme defects require hepatic ALAS1 induction before acute attacks develop.
edges.target: Hepatic ALAS1 Induction and Acute Precursor Overproduction

edges.description: FECH reduction or erythroid ALAS2 gain of function alters the same heme biosynthesis pathway and produces protoporphyrin IX accumulation.
UROS loss reduces uroporphyrinogen synthase activity and creates a CEP branch with erythroid porphyrinogen accumulation and erythrocyte damage.
edges.target: Erythroid Uroporphyrin I Accumulation and Hemolysis

edges.description: ALA and PBG accumulation precedes acute porphyria symptoms.
Photoreactive porphyrin or protoporphyrin accumulation drives light-triggered cutaneous injury.
ALA and PBG precursor excess is associated with chronic tubulointerstitial kidney injury in AIP.
Excess protoporphyrin eliminated through the hepatobiliary system can cause liver dysfunction.
ALAS1 induction increases ALA and PBG, the primary biochemical markers and toxic precursors of acute hepatic porphyrias.
edges.source: Hepatic ALAS1 Induction and Acute Precursor Overproduction

edges.description: Protoporphyrin IX accumulation causes severe light sensitivity and, in a minority, liver complications.
Excess PPIX elimination through hepatobiliary pathways can produce liver dysfunction and rare liver failure.
Photoreactive uroporphyrinogen and coproporphyrinogen accumulation elicits visible-light phototoxicity.
edges.source: Erythroid Uroporphyrin I Accumulation and Hemolysis

edges.description: Porphyrinogen-mediated erythrocyte damage causes chronic hemolytic anemia in CEP.
edges.source: Erythroid Uroporphyrin I Accumulation and Hemolysis

edges.description: Severe CEP can include cytopenias beyond hemolytic anemia.
edges.source: Erythroid Uroporphyrin I Accumulation and Hemolysis

edges.description: Chronic CEP phototoxic injury can involve the cornea.
intermediate_mechanisms: Recurrent visible-light phototoxic injury and scarring of exposed tissues.
edges.source: Erythroid Uroporphyrin I Accumulation and Hemolysis

edges.description: Acute hepatic porphyria attacks commonly present with abdominal pain.
Acute neurovisceral attacks include gastrointestinal symptoms such as vomiting.
Acute hepatic porphyria attacks can include neuropathy.
Phototoxic porphyrin or protoporphyrin accumulation manifests clinically as severe cutaneous photosensitivity.

Hepatic ALAS1 Induction and Acute Precursor Overproduction

edges.source: Bone marrow transplantation for severe CEP
edges.target: Erythroid Uroporphyrin I Accumulation and Hemolysis

edges.description: Increased urinary ALA reports hepatic ALAS1-driven precursor overproduction in acute hepatic porphyrias.
edges.source: Hepatic ALAS1 Induction and Acute Precursor Overproduction

edges.description: Increased urinary PBG reports acute hepatic porphyria precursor overproduction downstream of ALAS1 induction.
edges.source: Hepatic ALAS1 Induction and Acute Precursor Overproduction

edges.description: Increased erythrocyte or plasma protoporphyrin reports protoporphyrin IX accumulation in EPP/XLP.

edges.target: Erythroid Uroporphyrin I Accumulation and Hemolysis

nodes.description: Severe abdominal pain is a core manifestation of acute neurovisceral porphyria attacks.

evidence.reference_title: Givosiran: a targeted treatment for acute intermittent porphyria
evidence.snippet: The acute hepatic porphyrias (AHPs) are a family of rare genetic diseases associated with attacks of abdominal pain, vomiting, weakness, neuropathy, and other neurovisceral symptoms.

subtypes: Porphyria due to ALA Dehydratase Deficiency

nodes.description: Corneal scarring can occur in congenital erythropoietic porphyria.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital erythropoietic porphyria: Recent advances.
evidence.snippet: In addition, patients have erythrodontia (brownish discoloration of teeth) and can develop corneal scarring.

nodes.description: EPP can cause liver dysfunction and rarely terminal liver failure because excess PPIX is eliminated through the hepatobiliary system.

evidence.reference_title: Afamelanotide Is Associated with Dose-Dependent Protective Effect from Liver Damage Related to Erythropoietic Protoporphyria
evidence.snippet: In addition to the most prominent symptom of incapacitating phototoxic skin reactions, 20% of EPP patients exhibit disturbed liver functioning and 4% experience terminal liver failure caused by the hepatobiliary elimination of excess PPIX.

nodes.description: Afamelanotide is an alpha-melanocyte-stimulating hormone analogue and approved protoporphyria treatment that increases light tolerance and improves quality of life.

evidence.reference_title: Afamelanotide for Treatment of the Protoporphyrias: Impact on Quality of Life and Laboratory Parameters in a US Cohort
evidence.snippet: Afamelanotide, an α-melanocyte-stimulating hormone analogue, is the only approved treatment for protoporphyria and leads to increased light tolerance and improved quality of life (QoL).

evidence.reference_title: Afamelanotide for Treatment of the Protoporphyrias: Impact on Quality of Life and Laboratory Parameters in a US Cohort
evidence.snippet: Among the patients who received ≥2 implants, the median time to symptom onset following sunlight exposure was 12.5 min (IQR, 5–20) prior to the initiation of afamelanotide and 120 min (IQR, 60–240) after treatment (p < 0.001).

nodes.description: Chronic hemolytic anemia is common in congenital erythropoietic porphyria and can become transfusion dependent in severe disease.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital erythropoietic porphyria: Recent advances.
evidence.snippet: Chronic transfusion-dependent hemolytic anemia is common and leads to bone marrow hyperplasia, which further increases porphyrin production.

nodes.description: Bone marrow or hematopoietic stem cell transplantation can be curative in severe congenital erythropoietic porphyria.
nodes.id: Bone marrow transplantation for severe CEP

evidence.reference_title: Congenital erythropoietic porphyria: Recent advances.
evidence.snippet: In severe transfection-dependent cases, bone marrow or hematopoietic stem cell transplantation has been performed, which is curative.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital Erythropoietic Porphyria: A Rare Inherited Disorder.
evidence.snippet: Stem cell transplantation remains the sole curative therapy for this exceedingly rare condition.

nodes.description: Chronic kidney disease is a major complication reported in symptomatic acute intermittent porphyria.

evidence.reference_title: High prevalence of and potential mechanisms for chronic kidney disease in patients with acute intermittent porphyria.
evidence.snippet: Chronic kidney disease occurred in up to 59% of the symptomatic AIP patients, with a decline in the glomerular filtration rate of ~1 ml/min per 1.73 m(2) annually.

nodes.description: Cutaneous and erythropoietic porphyrias cause photosensitivity through accumulation of photoreactive porphyrins or protoporphyrin in skin, plasma, erythrocytes, or hepatobiliary tissues.

evidence.reference_title: Afamelanotide for Treatment of the Protoporphyrias: Impact on Quality of Life and Laboratory Parameters in a US Cohort
evidence.snippet: Erythropoietic protoporphyria (EPP) and X-linked protoporphyria (XLP) are rare disorders of heme biosynthesis characterized by severe cutaneous phototoxicity.

nodes.description: Photosensitivity is a core feature of cutaneous and erythropoietic porphyrias and may also occur in variegate porphyria or hereditary coproporphyria.

evidence.reference_title: Afamelanotide for Treatment of the Protoporphyrias: Impact on Quality of Life and Laboratory Parameters in a US Cohort
evidence.snippet: Erythropoietic protoporphyria (EPP) and X-linked protoporphyria (XLP) are rare disorders of heme biosynthesis characterized by severe cutaneous phototoxicity.

nodes.description: Dersimelagon is an investigational oral MC1R agonist being developed for EPP and X-linked protoporphyria.

evidence.reference_title: Illuminating Dersimelagon: A Novel Agent in the Treatment of Erythropoietic Protoporphyria and X-Linked Protoporphyria
evidence.snippet: However, the emergence of innovative therapies, such as dersimelagon, is reshaping the therapeutic landscape for these conditions.

evidence.reference_title: Illuminating Dersimelagon: A Novel Agent in the Treatment of Erythropoietic Protoporphyria and X-Linked Protoporphyria
evidence.snippet: Both of these abnormalities result in the buildup of protoporphyrin IX, leading to excruciating light sensitivity and, in a minority of cases, potentially fatal liver complications.

readouts.interpretation: Increased erythrocyte or plasma protoporphyrin reports protoporphyrin IX accumulation in EPP/XLP.

nodes.description: EPP and X-linked protoporphyria involve reduced FECH activity or increased erythroid ALAS2 activity, producing protoporphyrin IX accumulation in erythroid cells and clinically severe visible-light phototoxicity.

evidence.reference_title: Illuminating Dersimelagon: A Novel Agent in the Treatment of Erythropoietic Protoporphyria and X-Linked Protoporphyria
evidence.snippet: Erythropoietic protoporphyria (EPP) is a genetic disorder stemming from reduced ferrochelatase expression, the final enzyme in the pathway of heme biosynthesis.

evidence.reference_title: Illuminating Dersimelagon: A Novel Agent in the Treatment of Erythropoietic Protoporphyria and X-Linked Protoporphyria
evidence.snippet: A closely related condition, X-linked protoporphyria (XLP), bears similar clinical features although it arises from the heightened activity of δ-aminolevulinic acid synthase 2 (ALAS2), the first and normally rate-controlling enzyme in heme biosynthesis in developing red blood cells.

nodes.description: Congenital erythropoietic porphyria reflects absent or markedly reduced UROS enzymatic activity in erythroid heme synthesis, causing uroporphyrin I and coproporphyrin I accumulation, erythrocyte damage, and visible-light phototoxicity.
nodes.id: Erythroid Uroporphyrin I Accumulation and Hemolysis

evidence.reference_title: Congenital erythropoietic porphyria: Recent advances.
evidence.snippet: Disease-causing mutations in either gene result in absent or markedly reduced UROS enzymatic activity. This in turn leads to the accumulation of the non-physiologic and photoreactive porphyrinogens, uroporphyrinogen I and coproporphyrinogen I, which damage erythrocytes and elicit a phototoxic reaction upon light exposure.

nodes.description: Givosiran is an ALAS1-targeted siRNA therapy for acute hepatic porphyria that reduces ALA/PBG and lowers attack burden, with monitoring for liver, renal, injection-site, and homocysteine-related adverse effects.

evidence.reference_title: Long-term follow-up of givosiran treatment in patients with acute intermittent porphyria from a phase 1/2, 48-month open-label extension study
evidence.snippet: Givosiran therapy reduced annualized rates of porphyria attacks and hemin use by 97% and 96%, respectively.

evidence.reference_title: German Real-World Experience of Patients with Diverse Features of Acute Intermittent Porphyria Treated with Givosiran
evidence.snippet: Givosiran is effective in preventing severe acute attacks and reducing the chronic health burden in patients with acute intermittent porphyria.

nodes.description: Inherited porphyrias arise when pathogenic variants reduce activity of heme-biosynthesis enzymes or alter ALAS2 regulation, creating subtype- specific bottlenecks in porphyrin metabolism.

evidence.reference_title: Update on the Porphyrias
evidence.snippet: The porphyrias are a group of rare diseases, each resulting from a defect in a different enzymatic step of the heme biosynthetic pathway.

nodes.description: Intravenous hemin or heme arginate is used for severe acute hepatic porphyria attacks to repress hepatic ALAS1 and lower precursor production.

evidence.reference_title: German Real-World Experience of Patients with Diverse Features of Acute Intermittent Porphyria Treated with Givosiran
evidence.snippet: The elimination of precipitating factors, hemin therapy, and pain relief are strategies used to treat porphyria symptoms, but are often reserved for patients suffering recurrent, acute attacks.

nodes.description: In acute hepatic porphyrias, a germline defect in one of four heme-synthesis enzymes becomes clinically active when hepatic ALAS1 is induced, increasing ALA and PBG production and precipitating neurovisceral symptoms.
nodes.id: Hepatic ALAS1 Induction and Acute Precursor Overproduction

evidence.reference_title: Givosiran: a targeted treatment for acute intermittent porphyria
evidence.snippet: The acute hepatic porphyrias (AHPs) are a family of rare genetic diseases associated with attacks of abdominal pain, vomiting, weakness, neuropathy, and other neurovisceral symptoms.

evidence.reference_title: Givosiran: a targeted treatment for acute intermittent porphyria
evidence.snippet: Pathogenic variants in 1 of 4 enzymes of heme synthesis are necessary for the development of AHP, and the onset of acute attacks also requires the induction of δ-aminolevulinic acid synthase 1 (ALAS1), the first and rate-limiting step of heme synthesis in the liver.

nodes.description: Acute hepatic porphyria subtypes can develop episodic neurovisceral attacks with abdominal pain, vomiting, weakness, neuropathy, and related symptoms.

evidence.reference_title: Givosiran: a targeted treatment for acute intermittent porphyria
evidence.snippet: The acute hepatic porphyrias (AHPs) are a family of rare genetic diseases associated with attacks of abdominal pain, vomiting, weakness, neuropathy, and other neurovisceral symptoms.

nodes.description: Acute porphyria attacks can include peripheral neuropathy.

evidence.reference_title: Givosiran: a targeted treatment for acute intermittent porphyria
evidence.snippet: The acute hepatic porphyrias (AHPs) are a family of rare genetic diseases associated with attacks of abdominal pain, vomiting, weakness, neuropathy, and other neurovisceral symptoms.

subtypes: Porphyria due to ALA Dehydratase Deficiency

nodes.description: Sunlight and visible-light avoidance, protective clothing, and related measures reduce cutaneous phototoxic injury in photosensitive porphyrias.

evidence.reference_title: Illuminating Dersimelagon: A Novel Agent in the Treatment of Erythropoietic Protoporphyria and X-Linked Protoporphyria
evidence.snippet: Traditionally, managing EPP and XLP involved sun avoidance.

nodes.description: Defective heme-biosynthesis flux causes subtype-specific accumulation of ALA, porphobilinogen, porphyrins, or protoporphyrin upstream of the blocked enzymatic step, generating hepatic, erythropoietic, cutaneous, renal, and hepatic complications.

evidence.reference_title: Update on the Porphyrias
evidence.snippet: They can be broadly divided into two categories, hepatic and erythropoietic porphyrias, depending on the primary site of accumulation of heme intermediates.

evidence.reference_title: Givosiran: a targeted treatment for acute intermittent porphyria
evidence.snippet: In 2019 the US Food and Drug Administration approved givosiran for AHP based on positive results from a phase 3 clinical trial of 94 patients with AHP who demonstrated a marked improvement in AHP attacks and a substantial decrease in δ-aminolevulinic acid and porphobilinogen, the primary disease markers of AHP.

evidence.reference_title: Illuminating Dersimelagon: A Novel Agent in the Treatment of Erythropoietic Protoporphyria and X-Linked Protoporphyria
evidence.snippet: Erythropoietic protoporphyria (EPP) is a genetic disorder stemming from reduced ferrochelatase expression, the final enzyme in the pathway of heme biosynthesis.

evidence.reference_title: Illuminating Dersimelagon: A Novel Agent in the Treatment of Erythropoietic Protoporphyria and X-Linked Protoporphyria
evidence.snippet: A closely related condition, X-linked protoporphyria (XLP), bears similar clinical features although it arises from the heightened activity of δ-aminolevulinic acid synthase 2 (ALAS2), the first and normally rate-controlling enzyme in heme biosynthesis in developing red blood cells.

nodes.description: Thrombocytopenia has been reported in congenital erythropoietic porphyria alongside anemia and leukocytopenia.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital Erythropoietic Porphyria: A Rare Inherited Disorder.
evidence.snippet: Laboratory investigations demonstrated anemia, leukocytopenia, thrombocytopenia, and elevated urine uroporphyrin 1 and coproporphyrin 1 levels.

nodes.description: Avoidance of unsafe medications, fasting, alcohol, tobacco, and other attack triggers is foundational for acute hepatic porphyrias; supportive care is needed during attacks.

evidence.reference_title: German Real-World Experience of Patients with Diverse Features of Acute Intermittent Porphyria Treated with Givosiran
evidence.snippet: The elimination of precipitating factors, hemin therapy, and pain relief are strategies used to treat porphyria symptoms, but are often reserved for patients suffering recurrent, acute attacks.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital erythropoietic porphyria: Recent advances.
evidence.snippet: CEP is caused by mutations in the uroporphyrinogen synthase (UROS) gene.

evidence.reference_title: Givosiran: a targeted treatment for acute intermittent porphyria
evidence.snippet: In 2019 the US Food and Drug Administration approved givosiran for AHP based on positive results from a phase 3 clinical trial of 94 patients with AHP who demonstrated a marked improvement in AHP attacks and a substantial decrease in δ-aminolevulinic acid and porphobilinogen, the primary disease markers of AHP.

nodes.description: Vomiting is part of the gastrointestinal neurovisceral attack phenotype in acute hepatic porphyrias.
